commentBody:
"You'll love my art restoration service or your Monet back!"

(I'll make a good Impression.)
Wonderful theme and cluing,  and easy solve.
"What's Eating Gilbert Grape" actor - LEONARDODICAPRIO

Delightful easy Tuesday puzzle.  Fun theme with STARVING ARTISTS.  And a lot of clues that are slightly misleading at first but make you smile when you get it.  Like "ESPY" when based on the clue you wanted it to be TONY.

Maybe I'm missing something, but is calling STARVING ARTISTS a pun the best description?  "STARVING" in STARVING ARTISTS means that the artist isn't eating well (because their income is poor).  I guess the puzzle is saying the painters are STARVING because they are painting pictures of food?  

I'm not seeing the pun here, but maybe I'm just being clueless.

David Connell (we miss you DC) had a hilarious story about someone in his church mangling the word MYRIAD by reading it as “mermaid”. I think it caused DC to laugh out loud during a service.

Meanwhile I was all set to put GOOGOL there, everyone’s favorite large number.

Once, that song about liking pina coladas did so permeate the airwaves that I do remember brainstorming making a short video adventure thriller entitled "Escape from the Pina Colada Song." In fact I still can't listen to it without inwardly grimacing. 

Starving artists! It's not really punny (as DJ also said) but it's kind of funny applied to those three. Too bad there wasn't room for Manet's "Dejeuner sur L'herbe" -- now that's a repast! 

Other things to like: INK SAC/TAT; MEH/YUCCA (both sound like verbal reactions to something), the competitive quad of DUEL/ANGER/TKO/DRAW and of course all the food related words that actually do make me a little peckish: MOCHI, SHAKE, FISH (with a little lemon ZEST).

And very pleased to see VESTRY rides again. It's been a while, and what a beauty of a word.

Re: 61D - IPS is technically, logically, and linguistically wrong. 

I know we've been through this before, but Internet Protocols are absolutely, 100% *not* addresses!

There's FTP, HTTP, POP, IMAP, and dozens of other protocols that make the internet work. There's even an addressing protocol for the internet, which leads to the term "Internet Protocol Address".  But your - or your computer's - address on the internet isn't an IP, it's an IP Address. So the correct answer to the clue could only be (despite the likely confusion with beers of a particular style) IPAS.

I spent decades in IT, and while we usually shortened the term Internet Protocol Address to "IP Address", I never heard anyone refer to a numeric network identifier as an IP. 

(You will not get me to agree to any other interpretation, so don't bother trying).
I love a puzzle with an art theme. Great fun with enough misdirection to keep me on my toes.

Strangely, two people I have never been able to keep straight crossed in this puzzle.  MONET/MaNET,  and ONEIL/aNEIL.

(Kidding, I don't know of a tall shortstop named Aneil, but then again I've never heard of Oneil Cruz, either.)

Could we call this a "picture perfect" puzzle? It was nice to explore a little of the art world this evening, and I appreciated the sassy revealer, more so because I'm currently on a liquid/soft diet while going through the process of getting dentures.

There were some very slight delays in my solve, but never very long, as it ended up being one of my faster Tuesdays. I'd heard of all the artists, and seen original works or representations thereof by all three of them.

Thanks, Killian!

When I was seventeen we went on a class trip to London and Paris and I saw the Louvre for the first time. I had read in a travel guide that the museum is massive and daunting and it’s impossible to see everything in a single visit, so I strapped myself in a supine position to a skateboard and hired a small team of locals to rocket me around the museum. After three fibula fractures we decided to stop but I’ll never forget looking up at the ceiling of the Louvre all day while being pushed around on my back. Couldn’t see any of the art on the walls in that position, but that ceiling was something else.
Very nice! Loved the theme. And one clue that really tripped me up was the clue for 'fish'! Well done

Too fruity?

Like more MEH than rewarding, my favorite FILL had gone wrong
So while my hubby was sleepin'

But me and The Grey Lady had fallen into the same old dull routine
So I wrote to the paper: Is this some BABYTALK fad?
And though I'm not Shortz' pony, I thought it wasn't half bad:,

I'm into OHARE champagne
Solvers DECRY a rebus and all NATICK clues- for G Slovacek they WENTAPE!

For those of you who are thinking of today's puzzle as a bit of an appetizer, here's another one that will really fill you up.

Sunday, October 18, 2009
“Ahead Of The Curve” By Elizabeth C. Gorski Edited by Will Shortz

Someone recommended this puzzle in the last few days in these pages and it's loaded with sumptuous works of art. It also has lots of spanners or almost-spanners and an unusual grid.

Take a bite and bon appétit!!

I struggled with this. 
Specifically the NW. 
I couldn’t figure out what kind of dancing it was. 
“It may be found at the end of the line” was clever, in my opinion.  

I think in one the few that didn’t care to watch all 873 episodes of breaking bad.  I don’t even think I got through episode 1. 

Actually this took me longer than last Friday or Saturday.

I’ve kind of got an issue with “idea” being the solution for “brainstorm.” Brainstorm as a verb is to come up with many ideas. Brainstorm as a noun is a session to come up with many ideas. There’s no way the answer can be the singular “idea.” This had to be “ideas” or “ideate” in order to fit the clue.
I am yet again asking if there is a reason why Australians are having issues accessing the midi crossword

Perfectly charming.  FISH also the last to fall.  

Been away for a bit and see we are still tweaking the comments format.  Not too shabby.  Had to dial up the magnification.  Now, please add the "Open all replies" button.

Why are still life paintings so often FISH and lobster and pears and lemons? 

Poor mangoes and kiwis get no love. (Mangoes would be difficult to paint.)
Deliciously fun Tuesday. I Lworded the revealer, starving artists. The artist/ art pairing's were clever.

Help, please.

Can someone please give me the link for tech support?

I completed yesterday's puzzle yesterday morning, but it's not giving me credit for continuing my streak.

Thanks.

This was clever and quick. 
Coincidentally or not, if you're left wanting more, there's a reference in a recent Gameplay article to an inspired art-inspired puzzle from 10/18/09, and the grid is it's own brilliant bit of meta-art.
So, is DRAW an Easter egg theme echo?
Many thanks to A.O. Scott for a truly wonderful introduction to today's puzzle.  A real gem!

Oh, that’s an elegant theme.  

Sure, it seems like an obvious conceit in retrospect, but consider that it's never been done before -- luscious low-hanging fruit just waiting over the decades to be noticed.  Heck, neither the gorgeous STARVING ARTISTS nor its singular has appeared in a puzzle in any of the major outlets ever.  

Enter Killian, and high props to him for seeing it and giving its puzzle the quality it deserves:  
•	Finding not only well-known artists with mealtime-titled works, but whose names are of lengths that fit grid symmetry.  
•	Including a pair of lovely vertical longs, I’M CONFUSED and SLOW DANCED (both NYT debuts), as well as a pair of lovely shorts in MYRIAD and VESTRY.
•	Nailing the Tuesday cluing difficulty level, where the brain has to jump in rather than nap through.  Yet well short of late-week opacity.

Plus, an answer that made my mouth water (MOCHI) and made it smile at seeing EDGY on the bottom border.

Primo, Killian.  Thank you for a splendid outing!
I got stuck on MANET/MONET. Although CLAUDE supposes MONET, the most famous "Luncheon" painting is surely Eduard Manet’s "Le Déjeuner sur L’Herbe." No contest!

Lovely puzzle.
Artfully constructed, ample wit.
Just one NIT.

From the column, discussing the “best plays” and ESPY:  “This is about sports, not culture.” 

For a culture that doesn’t include sports, I think you need to look to AGAR, not the good old USOA.


There may be no accounting for taste, but maybe bean-counters could estimate each painter’s caloric deficit and gastric capacity?

I noticed yesterday that the comments looked different yet again, but it was late in the day, so I didn't say anything about it. I now see that a few people have mentioned it, but for a while, there was nothing, so I wondered at first if I unwittingly did something to my settings.

Now it seems that the font is bigger, and might be a slightly different one, but it's still sans serif, and it's hard to tell if a commenter is Al as in Albert or Alfred, or AI as in artificial intelligence. This is significant because some people show wrong guesses in all caps except the incorrect letters.

I also see that "Close Replies" was moved a bit away from "Reply" and "Recommend," and "Replies" (often with a number) was moved away from "Recommend" as well. This is a good change, because not just once did I recommend a post unintentionally when I was just trying to open the remaining comments.

I still don't see the purpose for "Close Replies." I think that option should be ditched.

Also, there should be a "Expand to Show All Posts" at the top, since many of us want to read all the comments straight through and not have to open threads one at a time.

I've been spending so much time opening threads that half the time, I forget to read all the comments (particularly the ones that were already showing before the ones I had to open).

Also, it's confusing when "Replies (number)" is sometimes followed by visible comments, and sometimes needs to be clicked on to reveal hidden comments.

Now, about the puzzle.

I'm not sure why painting pictures of people eating makes you a STARVING ARTIST.  I just don't think the connection is there.

That is all.

No, wait a minute. I gotta say more. This is Killian Olson's second puzzle. His first one, on 11/26/24, was also a Tuesday, in which the revealer was AUTO FILLED, and the four sets of shaded squares in the theme answers were car makes. That one was a much more solid theme, and one that showed that he has potential. Today, the revealer didn't quite match up with the theme answers, because, as I said, painting buffets and luncheons does not make one a STARVING ARTIST.

Some commenter just lamented the fact that Liz Gorski hasn't appeared in the NYT lately (in fact, for a decade). She has had several puzzles in the New Yorker, in fact, as recently as last Monday. It was mentioned, with great fanfare, in an end-of-year column within the last few years, that something like 80 new constructors debuted that year, as if that was something fantastic. But at what price?  I'd rather see Liz Gorski in the NYT than some guy who produces one good puzzle, and then one so-so one.

Do you think this might have anything to do with the difficulty drop?

Enjoyed this one. The theme was minimal but solid, and the grid included some interesting entries: YUCCA, MYRIAD, IMPROV, DARKWEB, MUDRUN, VESTRY.

Seemed just about right for a Tuesday, at least to me.
Another crossword, another time I have to remember whether it's Manet or Monet

Typical Tuesday workout for me. I actually remembered a couple of the painters from the clues, and worked out the third one fairly easily. Just a bit of a furrowed brow over the reveal, but no big deal. And... puzzle find today. A Wednesday from March 4, 2020 by Tracy Gray. I might have done this one, but of course had completely forgotten it. Anyway... Four theme answers in that one (as they appeared): ZODEFENSE HOWOEARTH TATUMOAL CLAUDEMOT And the the reveal: "Outdo... or a hint to entering four answers in this puzzle." ONEUP And you can probably guess as to how that worked. Here's the link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=3/4/2020&g=60&d=A ...
Monet, Manet, Tippy Tippy Day Day...have a great day all.

I enjoyed this, got a big smile when I got to the revealer. My first thought when LEONARDO DA VINCI was clued was, can’t they get a harder clue for him even on a Tuesday? But of course that was part of the theme. I thought this was a reasonable difficulty for a Tuesday otherwise and had to get the other artists with some crosses. And finding all of them with symmetrical lengths was very artful. I disagree with Steve L, I think this was more than so-so, and I don’t care whether there are more established constructors or newer ones, I just want good puzzles and good clueing. I’ve seen puzzles from established constructors which were so-so and dazzling ones from newer ones. It takes all kinds.

I hate to go against the grain (who am I kidding; I love to go against the grain), but my review of this puzzle is:  MEH, with a bunch of NITs.

Starting with the positives:  a MYRIAD of good fill, a couple of nice misdirects in the cluing, the longer non-theme answers are fresh, without being too difficult.  This has already been discussed by the columnist and earlier commenters.

But the theme/revealer???
Paint me unimpressed.
Are we really supposed to think of an artist who doesn't have enough money for food, but does have enough money for paint, making pictures of food as a way of satisfying his cravings?
And the Manet painting is not  called "Luncheon", but "Le Déjeuner sur l'herbe" or "Luncheon on the Grass".  It was originally titled "The Bath"; not a whole lot of food or eating going on, although there is that oddly nude woman in the foreground.
And Cezanne's "Buffet" is referring to a piece of furniture, not an all-you-can-eat array of dishes.  There are a few pieces of fruit and a stack of (unappetizing cookies), but I don't think that will satisfy anyone's hunger.

Am I taking the theme too seriously?  
Perhaps.
Is this just a lot of NITs?  
Yes, but that is the stuff that reduces a good puzzle to the mediocre, for me at least.

Way-hay - I did all of this without any look-ups or reveals - a very rare gold star.
The crossers were kind regarding the sport and the US term 'ATTA' although I'm used to Grammy/NANA now. 
How pleasant for me!

Not a fan. 

Boring and unsolvable theme answers that take up too much space in the puzzle. If you don’t know painters, the constructor says, good luck 😏. Too much trivia overall.
Huh

They were all meals and they were all paintings.  I knew that this was going to be one of those early week puzzles I enjoy the most: both halves of the answer would be relevant to the revealer.  And I was consumed by curiosity as to what the revealer would be.

I set the puzzle down on the floor where there was no chance I'd see the revealer clue ahead of time.  I toyed with both halves of a possible answer:  PAINTING, OIL, CANVAS, ART, or PICTURE had to be paired with MEAL, FOOD or DINING.  I thought and thought.  And the only thing I could come up with was (wait for it!)...

THE ART OF THE MEAL

Nope.  That's not it.  And while I don't think you have to be STARVING to enjoy a supper or luncheon or buffet, I do think that the theme of this puzzle is absolutely ADORBS.  It created the kind of curiosity in me that I always hope for -- and I really, really enjoyed it for that reason.
Clever theme and fun, quick fill.

For all them who confuse Manet and Monet--you're in good company: an insomniac Ogden Nash did as well: "Oft in the stilly night, When the mind is fumbling fuzzily, I brood about how little I know, And know that little so muzzily. Ere slumber’s chains have bound me, I think it would suit me nicely If I knew one-tenth of the little I know But knew that tenth precisely. O Delius, Sibelius, And What’s-his-name Aurelius, O Manet, 0 Monet, Mrs. Siddons, and the Cid. I know each name Has an oriflamme of fame. I’m sure they all did something, But I can’t think what they did. . . . . . . Gently my eyelids close; I’d rather be good than clever; And I’d rather have my facts all wrong Than have no facts whatever." (1947--Full poem: https://www.newyorker.com/magazine/1947/06/21/who-did-which-or-who-indeed)

I knew as soon as I started that this was going to be a fun puzzle, and it was! Not too easy and not too hard. I still had a couple of lookups (mostly for spelling0, but considering I've only been doing the Tuesday puzzles for a couple of months, I thought that was pretty good. And I love the theme! I'm an art buff, and so this was really enjoyable. Thanks for a great Tuesday puzzle!
Ok - so I had to look up the shortest short-stops in MLB history. While not THE shortest ever, I fondly remember watching Freddie Patek, 5'5", with the Pirates when I was a kid.

Cute theme, some of the cluing felt a bit clunky but altogether fairly breezy. The main thing I want to say is that Rupert Holmes, the guy who wrote “Escape (The PINA Colada Song)” is actually worth a deep dive. He has written some pretty interesting stuff. I mainly know his music. Some of it is a little cheesy but it’s definitely unique. Check out Lunch Hour: https://youtu.be/CMyeEQHOzgE And he’s also a successful novelist and playwright, although I don’t know much about his work in those disciplines.

Ludington has one of the best ice cream parlors in the country, it is great to be reminded. Also, again, a shout-out to Rupert Holmes. Best known for his music, he has written some impressive and fun mystery books.

I think BRAINSTORM Is a great concept. To me, one of its meanings is a bunch of people sitting around and trying to come up with a solution, invention, concept, etc. One person says something, which is a snippet of an idea, and another person is inspired by that and adds to it. Ideas fly around and provoke new creativity. It's a good thing to do, maybe and similar to what goes on here in the comments.  

Here's what Wikipedia says:

[Brainstorming is a creativity technique in which a group of people interact to suggest ideas spontaneously in response to a prompt. Stress is typically placed on the volume and variety of ideas, including ideas that may seem outlandish or "off-the-wall". Ideas are noted down during the activity, but not assessed or critiqued until later. The absence of criticism and assessment is intended to avoid inhibiting participants in their idea production.[1] The term was popularized by advertising executive Alex Faickney Osborn in the classic work Applied Imagination (1953).]
I guess technically the clue is correct, but Oneil Cruz hasn’t played shortstop for a couple of years.

Unrelated to today's puzzle (which I enjoyed, BTW) but flashing back a bit, I just learned that Space Force are launching a GPS satellite christened Hedy Lamarr, which is a pretty cool tribute. I also learned that she co-invented frequency-hopping transmission. That tech was originally for guiding torpedoes, but later used in military comms, with which I am intimately familiar. Thank you, ma'am.

We went to Paris when my son was 12. Husband, daughter(17), and I all reveling in a week-long art lovers orgy. He was very well behaved. But on the last day, he stood in the middle of the Louvre and looked us all in the eye and said "I hate art!!! ALL ART!"

Fun, quick solve!
Bit of a tangy Tuesday.  Nice little surprise.
Good Tuesday puzzle. Thanks!

Cute little theme for a Tuesday. I put DUEtS for the Hamilton clue. Finished the puzzle with something wrong. tED down didn’t seem obviously wrong and had to go through every clue looking for my mistake. I’ve never seen Hamilton but I assume those DUELS are also DUEtS? Unless the seconds are singing too.

How is it that the mighty NYT can't seem to get their Midi puzzle link under control?

Half the time it's not there, and 90% of the time it's there it leads to a "Page not found" error.

This is just straight links and HTML.  I can think of no reason this would be beyond the ability of a high school programmer.

And yet?  It's been like that for at least a couple of weeks.  Or should I say "weaks".  

Maybe part of the puzzle for the users of puzzles page  is to help the NYT get their tech department solve simple problems.
This one was lots of fun!

One late puzzle find. A Thursday from August 10, 2015 by Jules P. Markey. Eight rebus squares in that one - all of them "AIR." Some theme answers: CH(AIR) PR(AIR)IEDOG IMP(AIR)ED CL(AIR)VOYANT UPTONSINCL(AIR) AST(AIR)ES CON(AIR) and... UPST(AIR)SDOWNST(AIR)S And the 'reveal' COMPRESSED(AIR) Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=8/20/2015&g=14&d=D ....

What a fun and pleasantly crunchy Tuesday puzzle! For a moment, I thought I'd need to resort to lookups, which I haven't done for an early week puzzle in a couple of years. I have heard of The Last Supper, and that answer was a gimme, but am unfamiliar with the other 2 paintings. I needed a lot of crossings to suss out the artists. Add to that some unique words (VESTRY, L-WORD, MUDRUN) and oblique clues (it may be found at the end of the line) and this is one of the better Tuesdays in recent memory. Thank you.

Ooh! And one more puzzle find. This one quite amazing. A Sunday from July 27, 2008 by Mike Nothnagel and David Quarfoot with the title "Going every which way." The rebuses in that one were UP, DOWN, LEFT and RIGHT and there were eight answers which contained two rebuses, and of course crossings that contained one of those rebuses. Some theme answers: (UP)STAIRS(DOWN)STAIRS (DOWN)THE(RIGHT)FIELDLINE (LEFT)A(DOWN)PAYMENT (LEFT)IT(UP)TOCHANCE ITS(RIGHT)(UP)YOURALLEY STANDING(UP)(RIGHT) SIT(DOWN)ANDSHUT(UP) (RIGHT)WHEREYOU(LEFT)THEM And there were more. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=7/27/2008&g=29&d=A ....

To AO Scott,

The Canadian Grammys are called the Junos, so the singular Juno could have fit, but would certainly not have been as clever.
Nice Tuesday puzzle! LWORD stumped me, I was trying to make a word out of LW...etc
Very enjoyable Tuesday puzzle!

I looked up "Nana awards"  before I figured out what was actually going on there, and apparently there are two actual awards going by that name-one from the NANA Regional Corporation for Alaska Native shareholders, and one is a design competition for operable glass walls. So that clue (sort of) works on multiple levels!

I will echo folks saying Brainstorm = Idea didn’t make sense. I do not argue that “brainstorm” is a noun, but rather that it does not fit the definition of “idea”. The noun of “brainstorm”, in my experience, has always been defined as a group trying to generate an idea, not the idea produced itself. I have never heard anyone say “I had a brainstorm” *in the same* way one would say “I had an idea”.  If I said “I had a brainstorm” I would mean “I took time trying to generate an idea”. 

I ultimately understood the clue in its context and crossings, but that is my nit for the day. Regardless, a very fun Tuesday!
Not a very relaxing or enjoyable Tuesday imo, more of a Wednesday or even an easy Thursday puzzle

Nyt dev team, I need you to remove the column from the in-app “browser.” I don’t think you realize how often I’m going between the puzzle and the column. Please let me open the column in a separate app so that I can move between the two without losing my place in either spot.

@Mike
If you get sued, you've got a Cassatt law!  You always make a painting look just like new, only Morisot .

@Mike

If my Dalí is baroque, can you fix her? She belonged to Magritte grandmother, but Dada threw her in the fountain. Que Seurat, Seurat.
@Mike The only difference between Manet and Monet is a Miro.

@Mike
I figured I'd throw this one into the hopper.  'Tis the Cezanne, after all.
@M. Biggen " Miro, Miro on the wall, who's the fairest ......."

@DJ Not a pun, but irony.  Those artists never were starving due to penury, and had good income… so what is said is not what is meant, in the sense of poverty.

But they painted food, and were starving for real food.
@Cat Lady Margaret I did put googol in there, until it became obvious it was wrong.

@Cat Lady Margaret 

I like GOOGOLPLEX better.

I see they've changed the comments format since I've been here last. Given me a blue J. Bluejay! Yasss. What font is that anyway? Courier bold? It's like Queer Eye for the Straight Guy gave Comments a makeover.  But I see they were not able to remove the gibbous face of Mike the Punster from Munster (and I mean gibbous in the nicest way), so there's hope yet.

Not a fan. Seems the font is a scoche smaller. I don't mind how they've somewhat muffled the number of reccos. But the whole thing seems like change for change's sake. Starting with nesting the comments. Meh. Yucca.

userLocation: RIP CBS Radio. Lies sell better.
commentBody:
@john ezra
With you on the formatting changes in general. You can still edit your icon, which I will do now.  But the defaults post-changes are certainly anodyne. I suspect it's part of the blandulation of the entire Games site. Don't do anything bold, interesting or (heaven forfend) disturbing. Warm milk for all!

@Times Rita I meant the font for the blue "J" or your beige "T." The font of the text I'm writing in now seems like standard Arial or Helvetica or something like that but it does seem a more charcoal black now that you mention it. And I think it might be a shade smaller but that could be an illusion.

@Grumpy  FWIW, I've never heard IP Addresses abbreviated as anything but "IP" or "IPs", been in IT either as an hobby or professionally since the early-mid 1990s. 

At any rate, if I'm going to be pedantic, technically your computer's address is the network card's MAC (my first guess here), the IP is assigned to a given MAC address on the network when it announces itself and asks for an address.

@Grumpy
Then why is/was there a popular website called whatismyip. Or a command called ipconfig.

commentBody: @Eric Also, I've really enjoyed Tony's columns the last two days.
@Francis I mix up MONET and Manet too,but my ONEIL mixup us with Oneal! English spelling is so weird. Why should Neal, Neil and kneel all be pronounced the same?

@Francis

This could be a breakout year if he stays healthy.

The Pirates will probably trade him away.  

Baseball needs a new minor league structure that includes  independent teams and relegation/promotion.

@BarbWho
Someone gave me a great tip to distinguish the first two: Manet was an extrovert and painted people, Monet was an introvert and painted nature

@Al  in Pittsburgh

Pirates fan?

Most of my life I've been a Pirates fan, even though I've never been to the city.

When I was a kid the there wasn't even a state that Colorado bordered on that had a major league team, unless you include the Kansas City Athletics and don't really understand how states and city naming work.

So I chose a team.  Pueblo was a steel producing town, called "The Pittsburg of the West".  We took that as a compliment, rather than the insult it was probably intended to be.

Anyway, when I was little I rooted for the Pirates through the dirty air, and gloried when they went to the World Series in the 70s.

I haven't heard much since then.  They still have a team? (Bitterly joking.)

ML baseball needs a lot of things before I'll given them any more of my money.

At the moment, the fact that the Dodgers outspends the lowest paid five teams in the league tells me everything I need to know about baseball.  Your comment about how your star may being trade bait if he gets good enough is depressingly familiar.

commentBody:
@Daphne

userLocation: RIP CBS Radio. Lies sell better.
commentBody:
@Francis
@Al in Pittsburgh
My Blue Jays are now among the big spenders, with Guerrero's half-a-billion-dollar contract the cherry on top. (I am still staring in disbelief as I type this.) I have cut my attendance at live games to one p.a. 
And I join you in my revulsion at the rotting decay that is sports gambling (and the dripping carrion of prop bets.)
The disparity between rich and poor teams must be addressed. No one needs to be paid millions of dollars per year -- not even the miraculously graceful and powerful inhabitants of the diamonds. I think that salary caps are exactly what is needed.
But please, not relegation. It's  confusing, it's collective punishment, and it punishes the wrong people: the fans and the city, when it should be punishing the owners.
Notwithstanding, I'm counting the days 'til Opening Day.
PS O-Cruz is like the Statue of Liberty; if he's anywhere in the field of view, you can't look at anything else.

@SBK in TO

Maybe think of it as MANet was an extrovert and painted MAN, while MONet was an introvert, MOANing about, painting nature.

@Francis

I'm a Yankees fan born and bred but I moved to Pittsburgh in the magical year of 1960.

Despite that disastrous beginning,  I adapted to rooting for the Pirates in the NL.  The Dodgers and Giants were no longer attractive in any way.

Now back in NY State I am barely aware of their weekly success or lack thereof.  Sports Center posts the scores and headlines.

@Al  in Pittsburgh
Never too late. Recovery is always possible, although you've certainly made it tougher on yourself, moving back to Addiction Central.
But since you're in NYS, perhaps head up to Buffalo to see the Bisons AKA the baby Blue Jays. I love Triple A stadia, and the Bisons play in a nicely refurbished (thanks, COVID!) facility. 
Bonus points: you can visit some homes designed by Frank Lloyd Wright, drop in on the surprisingly good art gallery, and (if your passport, race, and gender/sexuality are government-certified), scoot up to Canada for a visit. commentBody:
@Andrzej

The two things you prized about "Better Call Saul" are precisely the reasons to watch "Breaking Bad."

@Matt

That may well be, but the whole theme and feel of the thing, as described and conveyed in what I've seen and read, seems totally unlike what I would enjoy. Not a single shot from the series made me feel like I'd like to see more of it.

Also, I've always been put off by all the hype around that show. I literally never like something everybody else loves: I hated (and that's not too strong a word here) some of the highest rated movies and video games of the past 20 years.

Then there are personal reasons: I don't think I could bear dealing with a theme revolving around cancer in the family. I've had too much of that in reality.

And finally, Rhea Seehorn as Kim Wexler made "Better Call Saul" for me, and she's not in BB.

I get what you're saying. In fact, I thought some of those things as I posted earlier. Still, I have this odd feeling about BB that I just can't shake. Maybe I'll reconsider.

commentBody:
@Bob T.
Look /very/ carefully under Andrzej's 1st post in this thread (it starts with "I've never watched..."). Tucked in on the left as obscurely as imaginable are the mystic words "Replies 1". Turns out that's a link 😯 and if you click it, lo! Matt's comment appears.
In my ongoing decryption of this place, I've grasped that if there's a ^ next to the number of Replies, it means all replies at the next level are shown.  (Of course, this tells you zero about lower levels.) 
If there's no ^ next to Replies, there are more replies to display one level down. Semiotically speaking, the opposite of the ^ should be a downward ^ but, well, it's NYT-Town, Jake.
Using a Chrome browser which I am determined not to update until forced to, on an Android.
Good luck.
Sorry, but as I use it as a noun, and as M-W defines it #1 as a noun, it is one (great) idea. https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/brainstorm

commentBody:
@Bill
Perhaps because there are no mango or kiwi trees in France. Plenty of pear trees, though.
For help with a technical issue, please send an email to our customer care team at NYTGames@nytimes.com.

@coffee
The full title of the painting was "The Luncheon of the Boating Party" and notably, There is a figure with a top hat in the background that was Charles Ephrussi, an ardent collector...  (If you read the wonderful _Hare with Amber Eyes_ you will get a fuller picture of Monet's friendship with that once-prominent figure....

@Mean Old Lady

Sorry--the Luncheon of the Boating Party was Renoir.

Late night wiki-walking: Manet painted Le Dejeuner sur L'Herbe which was a bit scandalous.

Monet painted The Luncheon mentioned in the puzzle which is a beautiful backyard scene with flowers and a picnic table.
Monet also painted Le Dejeuner sur l'Herbe, inspired by Manet's painting. 

A commenter here said the artists in the puzzle were not starving. From my research, although Monet owned property and sometimes could hire servants, he was also beset by financial problems and creditors at times. Some of his paintings were seized to pay debts, but one of his patrons bought them back. We all know the famous water-lily paintings from his home at Giverny.

Now on to research Cezanne's finances.

By the way, for a really interesting food painting, check out The Potato Eaters by Van Gogh. He said it was his favorite of all his own paintings.
@Mean Old Lady MOL, the boating party (at the Phillips in DC) is Renoir. Monet's Luncheon is an intimate domestic painting: https://sammlung.staedelmuseum.de/en/work/the-luncheon

Linda Jo,
No [High dudgeon] for you, I see.

@Steve L
HELP ME.

I cannot see all the replies on posts with more than 3 replies. I see that there are 5 replies to Mike's pun, but I can only see three. There is no option to expand the rest of the replies.

commentBody:
@Steve L
Have people forgotten the origin of the expression of  'starving artist'? 
It began with the cliché of the artist living in his (it was always a man in the cliché) bare, chilly, tiny garret, saved into habitability only by its access to a north light. The gaunt tenant goes without food to buy paints and canvas, so overwhelming is his need to produce art.
Fast forward and, with the stereotype firmly established, you get the Starving Artists cheap-o art company. I remember the cheesy commercials promoting their crummy 'art' produced by genuine Starving Artists (TM).
So the theme here struck me as perfectly humorous.

@Rich in Atlanta And one more puzzle find. A Sunday from January 28, 2018 by Priscilla Clark and Jeff Chen with the title "Surprise endings." I won't mention the clues - just some of the theme answers: THECOLOROFMONET BEVERYLYHILLSCOW THEBIGCHILI LICENCETOKILT TAXIDRIVEL And there were more. Here's the link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=1/28/2018&g=56&d=A ....

Hi @Rich in Atlanta,

We’re new here….can you please elaborate how and why you find old puzzles to comment on the current NYT crossword? Just curious…thanks:)

@The X-Phile

Steve L had the same reaction, but personally I think you both are taking it too seriously. I think it’s funny that a “starving artist” may think about and paint a food scene—maybe even set up a food scene deliberately so he can eat it later. It’s just a bit of a fun image/association. Agreed it wouldn’t be the most witty theme for later in the week, but pair that with a solid Tuesday grid and fair clueing and I think this at least a better than average early week entry.

@The X-Phile But it's a CLAUDE MONET painting. There are two with the same name, and confusingly, the other is by Edouard Manet. The MONET one has a lot of food in clear sight, and is plainly a painting of a picnic: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Le_D%C3%A9jeuner_sur_l%27herbe_(Monet,_Paris) The Manet one is, uh, a little less so: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Le_D%C3%A9jeuner_sur_l%27herbe Since Wikipedia titles the article on Manet's painting without qualification, but adds MONET's name to the title of the other article, I assume the Manet is the more famous one. Manet's painting came first, and MONET's was a response to Manet's. But for this puzzle, it's the other one.

@SP

Some theme ideas are just 36A.

@The X-Phile
The painting is The Luncheon by CLAUDE MONET, not Manet.

@Steve L
Thanks for the details! I had seen the Manet at the Orsay but hadn’t paid much attention to it.  The story behind it and the critical reception made for a good read over my coffee!

@Steve L

Monet did two paintings. One is Le Déjeuner, which is referenced in the puzzle--a beautiful backyard picnic luscious with flowers.

The other is Le Déjeuner sur l'Herbe, which was inspired by Manet's painting of the same name.

Thank you, 2 a.m. Wikipedia.

@The X-Phile

We certainly had different reactions today!

But I was simply happy to correctly fill in the full names of the artists. I even needed to think: MANET or MONET? Was almost certain that MONET was CLAUDE, and ONEIL made more sense than ANEIL.

And, as I noted in my post, I found some of the non-thematic entries interesting.

@The X-Phile - What didn’t rock your boat gave me a completely different reaction. I laughed out loud when the revealer entry emerged. This is a lighthearted sport for me, and a morning chuckle is a big bonus. Kudos to the constructor.

@The X-Phile
1) it's Monet's Luncheon, not Manet's l'herbe
2) "STARVING ARTIST(S)" is very much in the language, and the clue says "punnily"; none of those three artists seemed to want for food.

Anyway I got a kick out of it but as they say YMMV.
Mañana será otro día.

@Steve L
I think there are two Monet Luncheons, an earlier one that's a domestic scene at table with a child and three women, and a later outdoor one which was a response to Manet's and I believe is unfinished.

@D

Your mileage may very but these aren’t exactly obscure painters. At one point Cezanne had the most expensive painting in the world, I think it’s third now.

@D
DA VINCI, MONET, and CEZANNE are three of the most well-known painters of all time. Even if you're not an expert on art, you surely know these names and can guess them from the crosses.
@Katie You’re so right, it’s all my fault. Therefore I’m not allowed to comment on my experience with the puzzle. My bad!
@SP Ah, suddenly it’s all clear now!

@D

I wasn’t trying to be snarky, as I said your mileage may vary, you are just not into art, I get it. Just pointing out that at least he made sure these were well known artists and no one too obscure.
@SP And, please do tell, what was the purpose in pointing that out as a comment to what I posted?
@D - User issue.

commentBody:
@All responders to D

Well, you did it again.  You've given D a chance to play the victim.

You should all know by now that D can say whatever he wants, but you are not allowed to make any response he doesn't like.

Let's all join in and set things straight again.

Repeat after me:  "Aw, poor D, never gets to say anything without being criticized for it."
@Anonymous I used to think something like that about Eva from Kentucky.

@D

Giving a contrary opinion in a respectful way, which I thought was the point of these comments. I wasn’t trying to be condescending, I wasn’t trying to say you are stupid, I certainly wasn’t saying you shouldn’t have posted if that’s how you felt. I was simply saying IN MY OPINION these were very famous painters and should be fair game in a puzzle like this. If you want to know why I felt the need to say that in more depth, it’s because I liked the puzzle and felt it was deserving of praise. You disagree, that’s fine we both got our points heard. Why have the ability to reply in these comments if we can’t make a counter argument?

@D

I think (therefore I am pretty sure) the issue is your  having the theme answers 'boring and unsolvable' be a fact rather than an opinion.

Myself don't know official titles of Monet and Cezanne works (though I might probly ID the painter) but I suspect most any literate Earth person has stumbled over The Last Supper.

And then there's help from the crosses, as well as the Aha fun of figuring things out.

Good luck with future xwp challenges!

@Nancy

I like your revealer better.

@Nancy,

Me too.

@Bill
My rule of thumb is, if a painting has a MAN (or a woman) in it, it's a MANet. If it doesn't, it's a MONET. "Luncheon on the Grass" breaks my rule, because it has people in it. TIL it was painted as a response to a work by the same name by Manet.
